section_title: INTRODUCTION TO ALGORITHMIC THEORY AND MODEL 6
content:
This lecture discusses the algorithmic aspects of Model number six, 'Universal Trading,' focusing
specifically on buy-side low resistance liquidity runs and fractals.

section_title: FRACTAL NATURE OF MARKET MAKER MODELS AND NARRATIVE LOGIC
content:
Market maker sell models and buy models are fractal, meaning they can appear and form on any time
frame. However, the logic of the narrative is crucial to understand why price should go up to a specific level.
Without this logic, the application of the models is incomplete.

section_title: TWO STAGES OF ACCUMULATION FOR PRICE RUNS
content:
When looking for a price run and being bullish, the first step is to go through time frames from highest to
lowest. The objective is to find some kind of narrative that would be utilized for the price to be drawn up to a
specific key level, identified as a premium array. There are two stages of accumulation when looking for a
price run from the market price.

section_title: UTILIZING THE BUY SIDE OF THE CURVE IN A MARKET MAKER SELL MODEL
content:
When looking for short entries and having sufficient range from the current market price to the anticipated
short entry level, the buy side of the curve of a market maker sell model can be utilized. This is basically
like the blue shaded area. Often, the first accumulation stage might not be captured. In such cases, traders
should look for the second stage of reaccumulation, which sets up a run up into that premium array. This
universal trading model, Model 6, uses the buy side of the curve of a market maker sell model.

section_title: THE UNIVERSAL TRADING MODEL AND NARRATIVE APPLICATION
content:
This is a universal trading model because of the numerous examples and variables. Over years of
experience, traders will learn the flexibility of reading price and utilizing this framework. An example from
the S&P E-mini Futures June contract for 2022 demonstrated accumulation and reaccumulation leading to
a premium array. Even without certainty on the exact premium array, the narrative of failing to make a lower
low, a shift in market structure, and being at a discount ahead of the 9:30 AM S&P opening, supported a
bullish bias.

section_title: IDENTIFYING OBJECTIVES AND APPLYING THE FRAMEWORK
content:
In the S&P example, the market failed to break lower at the 8:30 AM news, indicating a shift in market
structure. After a retracement into a fair value gap (even overshooting it), confidence remained that the
trade would likely pan out because there was a reason for it to go to a premium, as the market was already
at a discount. The framework of accumulation and reaccumulation was used. Without certainty of the exact
upside objective, a nice easy objective a fair value gap below an old high was used, which happened to
get run out later on. This highlights the application of Model 6, using the buy side of the curve of a market
maker sell model.

section_title: FOUNDATIONS FOR ALGORITHMIC THEORY AND ENTRY STRATEGIES
content:
The algorithmic theory videos are designed for those who have already studied the content and been
under mentorship for at least a year. It's crucial to go through all time frames each week to find price runs
that have a nice sell-off or a rally from them beginning at a specific price point, then backtrack to see if a
market maker buy or sell model is linked to that. This is typically the case.
- When looking at the first stage and second stage accumulation before the turning point of a market sell
model, the market maker accumulation phase can be used.
- Entry strategies can include the YouTube model (Model 13 entry strategy) or a simple fair value gap.
- Generally, the objective should be known where price is reaching for, some kind of a target, some kind
of a premium array.

section_title: MENTORSHIP INSIGHTS AND DEVELOPING TRADING APTITUDE
content:
For those who rushed through the content, especially in 2021, these algorithmic theory videos may feel
incomplete or disjointed due to insufficient prerequisite knowledge. These videos represent the
conversation one would have in a workshop, detailing how components fit together, what to look for, what
not to look for, and how to navigate. Prior knowledge is essential for understanding the casual discussions
and best advice. All concepts fit together, but new traders might struggle to connect them. Frustration with
this process can hinder progress.
- Continuously press into it.
- Backtesting and seeing examples.
- Journaling and annotating charts (the number one thing).
The concepts work, and this model is very close to ICT's actual trading model.

section_title: PYRAMIDING ENTRIES AND MARKET MAKER MODEL LOGIC
content:
ICT's teaching models are used to demonstrate concepts within the created language, without divulging
too much of his actual trading model. The logic behind market maker buy and sell models, as well as
entering and pyramiding entries, is clearly visible in his examples. Pyramiding entries are executed when
opportunities arise.

Pyramiding is not always possible. If the market has already run up at least above 50% of the anticipated
run from its starting point to a premium target, and then retraces, it suggests only one stage of
accumulation. In this scenario, pyramiding is not feasible. For pyramiding, at least two stages of
accumulation are needed.
- If going long and getting an entry, and then another entry, pyramiding is possible.
- If an initial long runs up, falls short of the target, and starts to retrace, pyramiding can be done again,
assuming the premium array for terminus has not yet been reached.
Pyramiding requires an 'unrealized range' still in the marketplace, meaning there's still distance from the
current price to the intended target. The goal is to find runs that hopefully provide a fair value gap,
institution order entry drill, a short-term sell stop run, and then a rally.

section_title: INTEGRATION OF CONCEPTUAL STRATEGIES AND PUBLIC EVIDENCE
content:
Conceptual entry strategies, money management, and pyramiding all come together with sufficient
experience to anticipate them before they form. While it may sound mythical, many public examples on
Twitter and YouTube demonstrate this. ICT always makes his examples public; there have never been any
secret private interpretations or examples shown only in the mentorship, ensuring all evidence is in public
record.

section_title: TRADING STYLES AND ASPIRATION VS. CONSISTENCY
content:
When at least two stages of accumulation are present and the outlook is bullish, traders may elect to only
trade the long side, avoiding shorts. For the 'aggressive dynamo,' the aspiration to buy and sell at extremes
(buying, selling near the high, shorting, riding it down to a low, covering, and buying again) is 'phenomenal
trading' but requires being truly 'dialed in.' Rushing this process for ego or competition should not be the
motivation.
- The motivation should be to find a setup that is repeating consistently from a specific level to another
known level.
- If there is only one stage of accumulation, it often indicates a parabolic price run, especially if bearish,
where price moves in a hurry to reach a level just to go down. This means pressing more on the sell side,
which will be discussed in Model Number Seven's algorithmic theory.

- INITIAL SETUP
An example from May 31, 2022, on the S&P E-mini Futures demonstrated prices consolidating. While
initially interested in relative equal highs, price ultimately went above and swept that. A bullish bias was
adopted because the sell side was not taken out; price went very close but did not break it. A fair value gap
was present.
- The strategy was to buy in the fair value gap with a stop-loss just below the low.
- Confidence remained that price would not violate the stop because it should have already done so if it
intended to go lower, and its slow, lethargic movement ahead of the 9:30 AM opening indicated a lack of
bearish momentum from the 8:30 AM news.
- After 8:30 AM, price went lower, then rallied, showing a short-term shift in market structure with a fair
value gap. This served as the entry point.

The market's return back to the original consolidation represents the first stage of accumulation. A trader
could be a buyer there with the expectation of running relative equal highs, or a best-case scenario (which
was not initially expected but could happen).
- The second stage of accumulation occurred here: a fair value gap, into which price dropped before
delivering to the premium array.
- After reaching the premium array, the sell side of the curve began, and price aggressively traded lower,
taking out the original consolidation and digging into a deeper pool of liquidity.

section_title: MARKET MAKER BUY MODEL AND FRACTAL PRICE SWINGS
content:
When focusing on the market maker buy model, specifically the buy-side low resistance liquidity runs and
fractals, the goal is to identify instances where the market was expected to turn down. Price trades down
into a level interpreted as a discount, then a measure of reversal is awaited (e.g., a shift in market
structure, a low-risk buy). This is followed by accumulation and reaccumulation (second stage), running to
clear the original consolidation for that liquidity. This pattern is fractal, appearing as a weekly, intraday, or
monthly profile. Everything is interchangeable, with reversed logic.

section_title: NARRATIVE AND DISTRIBUTION IN FRACTAL PROFILES
content:
All these price swings are fractal, but the expectation, or narrative, defines their purpose. For instance, if a
market consolidates, breaks, and retraces, the question is: is it retracing to go higher than this, or is it
retracing just to go short? If it's retracing just to go short, that's:
- Distribution first stage
- Distribution second stage
Upon reaching a specific level, evidence is sought that price basically proves it wants to go higher, taking
place in the form of a short-term market structure shift (e.g., utilizing Model 4 from the YouTube channel to
make it simple and simplistic). Then, evidence is awaited that shows it wants to go higher. Every time there
is a point of up candles on the sell side of the curve, that means the lowest point to the left here is a point
at which there's up closed candles or what would be expected as up closed candles.
